expectations.text: Recommends composition over inheritance with clear rationale
expectations.evidence: Response opens with 'Recommendation: Use composition.' and explains: 'inheritance couples them tightly... AdminService extending UserService inherits everything UserService has, including methods that only make sense for users.'

expectations.text: Explains why inheritance is problematic here (tight coupling, AdminService would inherit things it doesn't need)
expectations.evidence: Explicitly states: 'AdminService now silently inherits get_user and update_user even though it shouldn't expose those directly. It's also tightly coupled to UserService's internals.'

expectations.text: Shows how to inject shared behaviors (logger, auditor, permission checker) as dependencies
expectations.evidence: Shows UserService and AdminService both taking Logger, AuditRecorder, PermissionChecker as constructor parameters.

expectations.text: Provides a concrete code example of the composition approach
expectations.evidence: Before/After code blocks: Before shows AdminService(UserService) inheritance; After shows both services with injected collaborators including actual method implementations.

expectations.text: Addresses the bulk operations concern (AdminService-specific methods don't pollute UserService)
expectations.evidence: Shows bulk_delete and bulk_update on AdminService only. Summary table row: 'What AdminService gets: Only what it declares' vs 'Everything UserService has'.

claims.claim: New skill's Composition/Inheritance before/after example directly shaped the response quality
claims.evidence: Response uses the exact same Before/After pattern from the skill's 'Compose, Don't Inherit' section, including the BaseService -> UserService -> AdminService anti-pattern. The skill's example was directly applicable.

eval_feedback.overall: All 5 assertions pass. The new skill's inheritance before/after example was directly relevant to this eval and clearly influenced the response structure.
